Q1: My study routine was honestly quite unconventional because I'm 28 and juggling a lot with limited resources. I'd carve out blocks of time whenever I could—early mornings before my part-time work, late evenings, weekends. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often set up practical exercises or create physical flashcards and study maps that I could interact with. The 22 hours I put in weekly were intense but scattered across whenever life allowed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I could maintain a steady rhythm, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to adapt. I learned to be flexible—if I missed my morning study window, I'd make it up somewhere else. Deadlines definitely created spikes in my activity, but I tried to stay somewhat consistent to avoid complete chaos.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check for new announcements, see if there were discussion posts to respond to, or access video content. I'm quite active in peer interactions, so often I'd log in specifically to connect with classmates and see what conversations were happening. Sometimes it was just to reassure myself I wasn't missing anything important, given my attendance isn't always great in person.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became almost like checking my email—a morning ritual. I think it happened naturally because I was enrolled in so many online courses that the platform became my main connection to my education. The peer support aspect really pulled me in; knowing my study group was active there made logging in feel less like a chore and more like checking in with friends.
Q5: The video content was incredibly valuable to me—I watched over 280 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. The discussion forums were also essential because connecting with peers helped me process concepts through conversation. As a kinesthetic learner, I'd often take what I learned from videos and immediately try to apply it somehow, which made that content stick.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't complete as many assignments as I should have—my completion rate wasn't great. Some readings I'd skim or skip if they felt too theoretical without practical application. When resources were limited to my situation or seemed redundant with what I'd already grasped, I'd deprioritize them. Time is precious when you're stretched thin financially and have to work alongside studying.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to real understanding or when it involved interaction with others. If I could physically do something with the knowledge—create something, discuss it, apply it—then it was valuable. Pure passive reading without any way to engage with it actively felt like a waste of my limited study hours.
Q8: Optional activities actually often felt more valuable to me because I chose them based on genuine interest or need. Required ones sometimes felt like hoops to jump through, even when they were useful. I gravitated toward optional peer discussions and supplementary videos because they fed my curiosity rather than just ticking boxes. That said, I probably should have focused more on the required assignments given my completion rates.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs to study during my commute or at work during breaks since I don't always have reliable internet access at home. I created physical study materials—handwritten notes, diagrams on paper, even models sometimes. My textbooks got heavily annotated and I'd study from those for hours without touching the LMS.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be deep in study mode with printed materials spread across my kitchen table, working through problems by hand, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also meet with my peer support group in person at the library, and we'd work through concepts together for hours. The LMS would just show I'd been quiet, when really I was intensely engaged.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, take detailed notes, maybe download it if possible, and then review my notes repeatedly offline. Same with readings—I'd access them once, print what I needed, and study from the hard copies. It was more practical given my limited resources and unreliable internet. One login might fuel a week of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, usually when work demands increased or when financial stress peaked. The high stress I carry sometimes made it difficult to focus even when I had time. During those moments, I'd still log in and try to stay connected through peer interactions, but deep engagement with new material was harder. My resilience usually pulled me through, but it wasn't easy.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were my biggest challenge—working to support myself while studying meant constant juggling. Fatigue was real; sometimes I'd log in late at night and just not have the mental energy to engage meaningfully. I'd end up watching videos passively or scrolling through discussions without really contributing. My LMS use became more superficial during those exhausting periods.
Q14: Yes, definitely. There were times I'd have a video playing while doing household chores, technically watching but not really absorbing. Or I'd open discussion threads and read through them without the energy to formulate a thoughtful response. The activity was there, but my brain wasn't fully present. It's the difference between going through motions and actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important for my grade. Part of it was prioritizing what felt more immediately impactful—like peer discussions that helped me understand concepts. Part of it was anxiety avoidance; starting an assignment meant confronting whether I really understood the material. I'd tell myself I'd do it after one more review, and then time would run out.
Q16: Deadlines created intense bursts of activity for me. I'd suddenly be very present on the LMS, checking requirements, accessing rubrics, sometimes frantically reviewing materials. Without deadlines, I'd spread my engagement more evenly across peer interactions and video watching. The deadline pressure was stressful but honestly necessary for getting certain things done given everything else competing for my attention.
Q17: Feedback was motivating when it came—knowing how I was doing helped me adjust my approach. Positive feedback energized me to engage more; constructive criticism sent me back to the materials to fill gaps. The peer support aspect meant I also got informal feedback from classmates, which sometimes felt more immediately useful than waiting for official grades.
Q18: The course structure with its online components actually worked well for my situation since I couldn't always attend in person. Having materials available asynchronously meant I could engage on my own schedule. However, the structure also made it easy to fall behind on assignments while still feeling engaged through videos and discussions. More scaffolding for assignment completion might have helped me.
Q19: The video content and discussion forums made engagement easier—they fit my learning style and schedule. What made it harder was when assignments required resources I couldn't easily access or assumed everyone had the same amount of free time. The peer support intervention was genuinely helpful; having that community made me feel less alone in struggling with the workload.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. High LMS activity through videos and discussions, but lower assignment submission. For me, it was about where I found value and what I had capacity for. Watching videos and talking with peers felt like learning; assignments felt like performance under pressure. Some students might also be exploring, trying to understand before committing to producing work.
Q21: That's interesting—it's almost the opposite of my pattern. I imagine those students are very efficient, maybe doing deep offline work and only using the LMS for essentials. They might have better external resources or study methods that don't require constant platform engagement. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing; you can learn deeply without logging in constantly.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they're clicking on, how much video they're watching. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see me pausing a video to work through a problem by hand, or the conversations I have with peers outside the platform. They definitely can't capture the offline studying that makes up so much of my actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, not just passively receiving it. It's when I'm asking questions, making connections, discussing with peers, and trying to apply what I'm learning. For me, engagement is physical and social—it involves doing and talking, not just reading and watching. It's a state of curiosity and investment in understanding.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from printed materials at my kitchen table, or the study sessions with my peer group at the library. It wouldn't capture the mental energy I brought to each video I watched, or the stress I was managing while still showing up. It also can't show how meaningful the peer connections were for keeping me motivated when things got hard. My engagement was much deeper than my digital footprint suggests.
